                        IntroductionWinter chill accumulation, defined as the cumulative exposure to cool temperatures during dormancy, is a key regulator of phenological development in temperate fruit and nut trees1,2 and is commonly quantified as chill portions1,3 (CP) or chill hours4 (CH). These crops require sufficient chill to transition out of winter dormancy and initiate their growth cycle. Each crop has specific chill requirements for proper development, known as its chill threshold. For instance, satisfactory growth typically requires approximately 38â54 CP for walnuts (Juglans regia), 36â65 for pistachios (Pistacia vera), 30â70 for cherries (Prunus spp.), and 55â60 for plums (Prunus spp.), depending on the variety5. Because many commercial cultivars of these crops operate near their chilling thresholds, modest reductions in winter chill (e.g., 5â10 CP) can be sufficient to shift a season from adequate to insufficient chilling. Inadequate chill can disrupt winter dormancy release and subsequent phenological development, leading to delayed flowering, irregular bud break, prolonged bloom periods, and result in uneven fruit development and ripening6. These disruptions reduce fruit quality, extend harvest periods, and lead to substantial yield losses. Conversely, when the chill requirements are fulfilled too early and are followed by unseasonably warm temperatures, bud break may occur prematurely, increasing the vulnerability of tender buds to spring frost damage7. Given these challenges, accurate chill prediction is critical for planning management interventions, such as the use of dormancy-breaking agents to promote uniform bud break under insufficient chill and frost protection strategies. Here, we evaluate the potential of subseasonal to seasonal forecasts (S2S) to provide chill sufficiency information with lead times of up to one season in advance.Concerns have intensified over declining chill accumulation in regions with Mediterranean-type climates characterized by relatively mild winters, such as California. Unlike cooler temperate regions, California operates within a narrow margin, where the difference between the required and available chill is small, leaving its specialty crops vulnerable to even a modest increase in temperature8. Several studies have documented this decline in chill. For example, Baldocchi and Wong8 estimated a loss of ~40 CH per decade. Luedeling et al9. projected that winter chill in Californiaâs Central Valley could decline by 30â60% by mid-century and up to 80% by the end of the century, relative to the 700â1200 CH observed around 1950. Such reductions could push many regions below the physiological thresholds needed for successful bud break, thereby reducing flowering and fruit set10. These studies robustly document long-term declines in winter chill but largely emphasize mean trends rather than year-to-year variability.Crucially, climate change is not only causing gradual warming but also amplifying the interannual variability of temperature11,12,13, a trend often overlooked in agriculture impact studies. This dual challengeâdeclining mean and rising variability in chill accumulationânarrows the buffer that historically protected growers from occasional low-chill years. In the past, when available chill exceeded crop requirements by a wide margin, minor forecasting errors had relatively limited consequences for crops needing a high amount of winter chill. Today, however, the margin is shrinking, and unreliable forecasts may result in costly management missteps.Despite extensive documentation of long-term declines in winter chill9,14,15, two critical gaps remain. First, most prior studies focus on mean trends, leaving year-to-year variability largely unquantified, even though it directly determines how frequently chill requirements are not met. Second, it is unclear whether widely used Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP) climate models capture not only the magnitude of observed chill declines but also their interannual variability. Addressing these gaps is essential for distinguishing long-term climate-driven chill risk from operational risk and for evaluating the reliability of climate information used in agricultural decision-making. These gaps motivate the present study, which integrates observed variability, climate projections, and S2S forecasts to assess winter chill risk across decision-relevant timescales.Year-to-year chill variability is often governed by minimum daily temperatures during the coldest portions of the diurnal cycle, so monthly averages may obscure short-lived cold extremes critical for crop physiology. For example, Jha and Pathak16 used monthly-averaged forecasts to evaluate chill variability, but such approaches may inadequately capture these extremes. This highlights the need to evaluate chill accumulation using daily-resolution observations and forecasts.Understanding long-term trends and interannual variability in winter chill is especially important for perennial crops, which require several years to reach full productivity and remain in production for decades. Aligning orchard lifespans and breeding timelines with projected changes in chill underscores the importance of targeting appropriate chill requirements in new cultivars, motivating the need for reliable projections of future chill conditions. Innovative approaches such as Speed Breeding, which uses controlled environmental conditions to accelerate breeding cycles, can shorten breeding timelines and support faster development of climate-adapted cultivars, particularly when integrated with marker-assisted selection and genomic tools17.More generally, adaptation strategies that integrate climate information with crop phenology and management can help mitigate climate change impacts on perennial crops18.In this study, we analyze 44 years of observed daily temperatures from GRIDMET (hereafter referred to as the observational data) to quantify both long-term trend and interannual variability in winter chill accumulation across major fruit and nut-producing regions in California. We evaluate whether current conditions are already approaching or crossing crop-specific chill thresholds by comparing the observed trends and interannual variability in winter chill with CMIP6 projections under a high-emissions pathway. This comparison allows us to assess whether the magnitude and interannual variability of observed declines are consistent with projections under an upper-bound warming scenario and whether the earliest emergence of chill-related risk aligns with timelines reported in previous studies.To assess whether state-of-the-art forecast systems can help growers prepare for the risk of chill deficit, we evaluate the performance of S2S forecasts from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF). Specifically, we examine how well the forecasts capture interannual variability in winter chill accumulation at both monthly and daily temporal resolutions, and how accurately they identify years with insufficient chill.Together, these analyses go beyond long-term trends to quantify how increasing interannual variability alters the frequency of years falling below critical chill thresholds, thereby complementing long-term climate projections by revealing near-term chill-related risk. The study focuses on high-value fruit and nut crops for which California is a leading U.S. producer, including pistachios, walnuts, plums, and cherries, all of which require substantial winter chill for optimal development19. These crops are not only critical to Californiaâs agricultural economy but also play a very important role in national and global food supply chains.ResultsObserved trends in chill accumulationAnalysis of the observational data reveals a notable decline in chill accumulation across major crop-growing regions in California over recent decades. During the baseline period of 1980â1990, average CP in southern California (below 37Â° latitude) was approximately 64 CP in pistachio and cherry-growing regions, and 65 in walnut and plum regions, with interannual variability reflected by standard deviations (SDs) of 4â4.5 CP and 95% confidence intervals ranging from about 61 to 68 CP across regions. Over the most recent decade (2015â2024), these values declined to roughly 60 CP in all four crop regions, with higher interannual variability (SDs of 6â6.4 CP, 95% CI: ~55â65 CP), representing a decrease of about 4â6 CPs relative to the baseline period (Figs.Â 1 and 2; and Supplementary Figs.Â 1 and 2). Linear trend analysis indicates a statistically significant downward trajectory in chill accumulation across all four crop-growing regions (Fig.Â 2), reflecting the influence of long-term warming. Based on crop-specific chill thresholds, calculated as the median of CP across all listed varieties by Pope5 (58 for pistachios, 47.5 for walnuts, 48 for cherries, and 57.5 for plums), the current trend suggests that many regions are approaching the minimum required chill thresholds for these crops. This poses a concern as full yield potential with quality fruit for these crops cannot be realized without sufficient chill accumulation.Fig. 1: Trends in winter chill changes across California from 1980 to 2023.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageThe map shows the rate of change (slope) in winter chill over the period 1980â2023 at each grid point, estimated using the TheilâSen estimator. Future projections are based on the SSP 5-8.5 Shared Socioeconomic Pathway.Spatially, the decline in chill accumulation over the period 1980â2024 is widespread across all four crop-growing regions in California, with 91%â95% of grid cells within each crop region showing negative trends in chill accumulation over time, based on Senâs Slope Estimator20. Statistical significance of these trends was assessed using the autocorrelation-adjusted MannâKendell test21,22. The proportion of grid cells showing significant declines (pâ<â0.05) in CP is 50% in pistachio regions, 57% in walnut regions, 53% in cherry regions, and 54% in plum regions.Rising interannual variabilityImportantly, here we find the emergence of a higher interannual variability regime in chill accumulation since the late 1990s, alongside long-term declines in mean chill. This is reflected in the 10-year moving SD of chill accumulation, which is substantially higher in recent decades than in the 1990s (Fig.Â 3) and remains persistently elevated thereafter. Although variability appears relatively stable after ~1998, the median rolling SD increased from approximately 4.3â4.6 during the 1990s to 7.1â7.2 in the post-1998 periods across all crops (MannâWhitney test, pâ<â0.01), corresponding to an increase of about 56%. This dual pattern of declining means and increasing variability increases the likelihood of years falling below the physiological thresholds required for optimal development of these crops. While linear trend-based trajectories suggest that median chilling thresholds may not be consistently crossed until later decades, the proximity of current chill availability to these thresholds already implies heightened vulnerability to episodic chill insufficiency under present-day climate conditions. Such risk has indeed materialized: inadequate chill accumulation (i.e., with CP falling below its critical median threshold) was observed in pistachio, cherry, and plum-growing regions during only 3, 0, and 2 years, respectively, in the first half of the observational record (1979â1998). In contrast, these numbers increased to 6, 1, and 5 years, respectively, during the latter half of the record (2004â2023; Fig.Â 2). Although inadequate chill has not been observed for walnuts, due to their relatively low chill requirement, years with chill accumulation just 5 CPs above threshold occurred only once before 1998, but the frequency has tripled in the years since. These results indicate that chill disruptions associated with low chill accumulations are already occurring due to increasing interannual variability and are becoming more frequent across California, particularly for pistachio and plum-growing regions, which experienced insufficient chill in nearly 30% and 25% of the years, respectively, over the past two decades, even though long-term trend-based threshold crossings are projected to occur later. Together, these results highlight that changes in variability play a critical role in determining when and how frequently chill insufficiency occurs and, therefore, must be considered alongside mean trends when evaluating climate change impacts on perennial cropping systems.Fig. 3: Increasing interannual variability in CP in crop-growing regions of California.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageThe figure shows changes in interannual variability of CP for crop-growing regions south of 37Â° latitude in California, based on GRIDMET data. At each time step, the SD of CP was computed across all grid points within the region using a 10-year moving window.Climate model simulations underestimating the riskWe evaluate whether long-term changes in the frequency of chill insufficiency risk are captured in the climate simulations from the latest generation of Climate Model Intercomparison Project, phase 6 (CMIP6). Because chill accumulation within a given year is strongly influenced by weather variability, our analysis focuses on changes in the frequency of chill insufficiency rather than predicting exact within-year outcomes. Across all four crop-growing regions, CMIP6 historical simulations (1981â2014) substantially underestimate interannual variability in chill accumulation, with the modelsâ SD roughly half that of observations (about 53% lower). As a result, CMIP6 models fail to reproduce a large fraction of observed chill insufficiency events at annual timescales when evaluated against median cultivar-specific chill requirements. In the historical period, CMIP6 captures only 0â2 insufficient chill years across regions, compared to 2â6 observed events depending on crop, indicating that roughly 60â70% of insufficiency occurrences are missed (Fig.Â 2). Because the median chill thresholds of walnut and cherry are lower than for pistachio and plum, historical chill insufficiency has been rare, occurring only once in cherry and not at all in walnut during the historical period.Climate projections under SSP5-8.5 starting in 2015 indicate a marked increase in chill insufficiency risk. Pistachio and plum-growing regions experience frequent insufficiency, occurring in 18â19 of the 26 years between 2015 and 2040, whereas walnut and cherry regions begin to show occasional insufficiency (one year by 2039), suggesting that these traditionally low-risk crops may transition toward more frequent chill insufficiency in the early 2040s or beyond if current trends persist (Fig.Â 2). Importantly, by analyzing GRIDMET observations, we find that these thresholds are already being crossed intermittently for pistachio, cherry, and plum under present-day climate variability, although trend-based analysis indicates that the transition to persistent chill insufficiency occurs later than indicated by isolated low-chill years (Fig.Â 2). This discrepancy arises because chill accumulation depends on short-term variability, which is not fully represented in CMIP6 model simulations when analyzed at annual timescale. Since CMIP6 models underestimate the observed interannual variability in chill accumulation, projections based solely on mean trend threshold crossings likely underrepresent the near-term frequency of insufficient chill years, particularly in walnut growing regions. Prior works based on climate projections have suggested that the ongoing warming will significantly reduce the accumulation of winter chill in California by mid- to end-century, with conditions expected to no longer support some of the key tree crops currently grown in the region9.Our results refine this interpretation by distinguishing between episodic chill insufficiency that is already occurring due to interannual variability and the longer-term shift toward persistent insufficiency indicated by declining trends. Together, these findings indicate that mid- to late-century timelines may underestimate the urgency of the problem, as physiologically disruptive declines in winter chill are already emerging prior to mid-century and are projected to intensify under continued warming, even if trend-based thresholds are crossed later in the century.We further investigate whether these trends are consistent across administrative boundaries. Analysis at the county level reveals a broadly similar pattern: insufficient chill accumulation has become more frequent across most counties over the past two decades (Supplementary Fig.Â 5).The finding has important implications for both agricultural adaptation and the use of climate model projections for agricultural risk assessment. First, it indicates that increased interannual variability can lead to earlier occurrences of chill insufficiency than would be expected from long-term mean trends alone. As a result, growers may need to implement adaptation strategies earlier than suggested by mean climate projections. These may include selecting low chill cultivars, applying dormancy-breaking agents, or considering shifts in production regions to maintain crop viability under a warming and more variable climate. Second, this suggests that CMIP6-based projections may underestimate near-term chill risk if variability at the colder end of the temperature distribution (e.g., below 7âÂ°C) is not adequately captured.Evaluation of ECMWFâs forecast skill for chill predictionOur analysis shows that 1-month lead forecasts (NDJ observationsâ+âFebruary forecasts) are highly accurate, with predicted chill in the four crop-growing regions within Â±10% of observed values for 42 of 43 years using ECMWF daily forecasts (MD) and 41 of 43 years using monthly mean forecasts (MM) (Supplementary Fig.Â S6). Applying a stricter Â±5% threshold reduces the number of accurate years to 30 (MD) and 28 (MM). Grid-level evaluation followed a similar pattern, with 89â91% of grids within Â±10%, dropping to 57â60% within Â±5%. For 2-month lead forecasts (ND observations + JanuaryâFebruary forecasts), accuracy declined to 39 (MD) and 35 (MM) years within Â±10%, and only 26 (MD) and 23 (MM) years within Â±5%. Longer lead times (3 and 4 months) further reduce performance at both regional and grid levels, with grid-level accuracy dropping from 55â73% (Â±10%) to 31â44% (Â±5%).The time series comparison of ECMWF hindcasts and GRIDMET-derived CP illustrates year-to-year differences between predicted and observed values (Supplementary Figs.Â 7 and 8). It is possible to observe how 1-month lead forecasts closely track observed winter (NovemberâDecemberâJanuaryâFebruary (NDJF)), CP across all regions, while forecast skill decreases with longer lead times.The anomaly correlation coefficient (ACC) between NDJF total CPâcomputed as the ensemble mean of daily forecasts from individual ECMWF members, initialized on 1 November (lead time extending to 4 months)âand corresponding estimates from the observed temperature data ranged from 0.54 to 0.57 across the four crop-growing regions. When using monthly ECMWF forecasts, with CP similarly averaged across all ensemble members, the ACC values are even lower, ranging from 0.48 to 0.52. These relatively modest correlations indicate that the ECMWF forecasting system has limited skill in capturing the interannual variability in chill for these crop-growing regions in California. We also computed ACC between monthly total chill from the ECMWF temperature forecasts and the observed data for lead months 1â4 (November to February), separately (Fig.Â 4). In the four crop-growing regions (pistachio, walnut, cherry, and plum), 94â95% of locations show ACCâ>â0.5 at the one-month lead (November), but this drops to only 1â3% in two-month lead (December), 0â7% in three-month lead (January), and to zero thereafter, indicating no usable skill beyond one month.Fig. 4: ACC between the total winter chill derived from the ECMWF forecast and observed temperature.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageThe figure shows the ACC between the monthly total chill derived from ECMWF forecasts and observed temperatures for November through February. Only regions common to pistachio, walnut, cherry, and plum are displayed.The limited gains in skill despite leveraging both increased temporal granularity and ensemble averaging suggest that improving forecasts of chill will likely require more than just higher temporal resolution or ensemble spread. One possible explanation for the limited skill is that dynamic forecasting systems often rely heavily on ENSO as a source of predictability, even though ENSO has minimal direct influence on subseasonal climate variability in California23,24. Even in strong ENSO years, Californiaâs climate anomalies often deviate substantially from expected patterns. We analyze the correlation between winter chill accumulation in four crop-growing regions and the Oceanic NiĂ±o Index (ONI) at various lead and lag intervals. Our results showed that chill accumulation during the NDJF period exhibits a weak positive correlation with ONI values from the next yearâs DecemberâMarch period (correlation coefficients ranging from 0.16 to 0.24), and a weak negative correlation with ONI values from the subsequent September-December period (correlation coefficients ranging from â0.20 to â0.26). In contrast, correlations with ONI values from the concurrent or previous year are generally weaker. We note that nonlinear ENSO effects, ENSO Modoki events, and multi-index predictors may also influence subseasonal climate variability in California, representing promising avenues for improving S2S forecast skill in future work.DiscussionTackling the sooner-than-expected decline in winter chillUnderstanding the implications of a sooner-than-expected decline in winter chill for the future cultivation of these crops in California is crucial. Addressing this challenge requires first understanding how reduced chill accumulation is impacting crop performance, how growers are currently responding, and evaluating the potential effectiveness of available adaptation strategies. Our study contributes to that effort by offering insights that can inform and enhance ongoing work in this area.In particular, our findings suggest that focusing solely on long-term mean declines may underestimate near-term agricultural risk. By showing that increasing interannual variability is already increasing the frequency of insufficient chill years, this study highlights a pathway through which chill insufficiency is emerging earlier than indicated by mean-based projections alone.Current varieties of major tree crops have limited tolerance for winter chill deficiency, with evidence suggesting they may endure up to about a 20 percent decline25. Historically, unusually warm winters, particularly in 1998 and 2013â2014, when CP dropped by about 25%, have already proved to exert significant impacts on crop production and revenue losses in the Central Valley25. As a result, cultivar innovation has emerged as a key adaptation strategy, particularly for high-chill crops. In cherries, experiments are underway to assess the sensitivity of existing cultivars such as Bings, Corals, Royal Hazel, Royal Lynn, Rainier, and Black Pearl located in the southern San Joaquin Valley to declining winter chill26. At the same time, lower-chill varieties such as Royal Lee, Minnie Royal, and Cristobalina are increasingly adopted in warmer regions, especially in Southern California27,28. Pistachio breeding efforts have produced cultivars like Golden Hills and Lost Hills with slightly reduced chill needs, inferred from the earlier bloom dates, but this has not been definitively established (UCCE Farm Advisor Phoebe E Gordon, personal communication). Walnut adaptation via cultivar replacement is slower, likely reflecting the historically low occurrence of chill insufficiency. This slower pace of adaptation is also reflected in the fact that cultivar selection in walnut has been primarily driven by yield, nut quality, and disease avoidance traits, with established cultivars such as Chandler remaining dominant in California (Pers. Comm. with UC ANR Orchard Advisor Kat Jarvis-Shean).Perennial orchard systems operate on multi-decadal timescales, with trees typically requiring several years to reach full production and remaining productive for decades. For example, pistachio, walnut, plum, and cherry trees reach peak production after approximately 7â25 years, while orchard lifespan often extends several decades, depending on the crop29,30,31,32,33. In parallel, the development of new cultivars requires 15â20 years when multiple breeding cycles are involved34. Given these multi-decadal timelines, cultivars initiated today would likely reach commercial production around 2040â2050, when chill accumulation is projected to average approximately 50 CP in these crop-growing regions. Breeding efforts should therefore prioritize perennial varieties' resilience to such low-chill and increasingly variable conditions.While chill thresholds are used here as indicators of dormancy fulfillment and risk, it is important to recognize that chilling and subsequent heat accumulation (forcing) interact in determining phenological development, with a well-established tradeoff between chilling and forcing requirements35,36. Incorporating this interaction would provide a more comprehensive representation of phenological responses, but it is beyond the scope of the present study.Despite this limitation, increasing interannual variability and the resulting uncertainty in chill accumulation highlight the need for actionable, in-season decision-support tools. In-season chill forecasts can offer an additional short-term adaptation tool, enabling growers to anticipate chill shortfalls and plan for the application of rest-breaking agents (Pers. Comm. with UC ANR Orchard Advisor Kat Jarvis-Shean). In California, cherry growers have already been using chill forecast tools, which work better for deciding when to apply dormancy-breaking agents9. Experiments are also ongoing to understand the effectiveness of rest-breaking agents for walnuts in different orchards under low-chill years37. For pistachios, chill forecasts are occasionally used to assess maleâfemale bloom synchronization risks.Under low chill conditions, dormancy-breaking treatments typically begin by at least a month before the bud break37, often by the end of January. This operational timeline makes forecasts with at least a 1-month lead time essential for decision-making. Our results indicate that a practical approach for in-season chill assessment is to combine observed chill accumulation through January with 1-month ECMWF forecasts for February. This hybrid approach leverages the higher skill of short-lead forecasts (e.g., higher ACC and lower RMSE) while retaining observed variability earlier in the season. Using this method, NDJF chill totals were within 10% of observed values in nearly all years (41â42 years out of 43), providing a reliable basis for forecasting chill conditions. Although longer-lead forecasts are desirable for operational decisions, their skill is currently limited; therefore, this 1-month lead hybrid approach offers a near-term, operationally feasible solution for growers.The chill modeling strategy we present here, forecasting chill one month in advance, allows growers to adaptively manage chill shortfalls and make informed decisions on rest-breaking treatments and other nuanced orchard management practices. For instance, delaying pruning can help avoid stimulating premature bud break in years with low chill accumulation38. Similarly, forecasts can inform irrigation scheduling and orchard floor management strategies aimed at modulating microclimates39,40. While some cherry growers have adopted microclimate-modifying practices such as shade netting (e.g., DayChill) to enhance chill accumulation, similar applications remain limited for walnuts and pistachios26,41. Finally, the updated information on long-term chill decline in the future from this study can support broader strategic planning, such as the potential relocation of orchards. These projections make it clear that relocation decisions should not be viewed as a distant future concern. These management and adaptation options are presented to illustrate the range of responses to declining winter chill, a quantitative comparison of their costs, feasibility, or long-term effectiveness is beyond the scope of this study.MethodsStudy areaOur analysis focuses on regions across California where walnuts, pistachios, cherries, and plums are cultivated (Fig.Â 5). We assessed chill sufficiency separately for each crop, specific to its growing areas. To delineate these areas, we used the 2022 Cropland Data Layer (CDL) from USDA-NASS, which provides land use data at a 30âm resolution42. Crop-Specific masks were created by aggregating the CDL data to align with the 4âkm resolution of the observational data. Chill sufficiency computation was then generated for each masked region at this 4âkm resolution. Additionally, we emphasized crop-growing regions in southern California (south of 37Â° latitude), where previous studies have reported chill insufficiency risks16,25. A county-level analysis was also conducted for the six counties with the largest cultivation areas for these crops.Fig. 5: Spatial distribution of crop-growing regions for walnut, pistachio, cherry, and plum in California.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageColored areas in the map represent regions where each crop is grown. The six counties with the largest crop-growing area for each crop are highlighted with bold blue outlines.DataDaily maximum (Tmax) and minimum (Tmin) temperatures from the observational dataset (GRIDMET) were used as the reference for historical observations in this study. GRIDMET provides temperature data at a 4âkm spatial resolution, available from 1979 up to the previous day. The dataset is developed by merging temporally rich data from the North American Land Data Assimilation System Phase 2 (NLDAS-2) with the spatially fine resolution (800-m) Parameter-elevation Regressions on Independent Slopes Model (PRISM) data43. It was validated against an extensive network of automated weather stations across the western United States, showing skill comparable to station-based interpolation methods. For consistency with the model forecast period, we selected data from 1981 to 2024.Model data from the ECMWF, consisting of six-hourly forecasts, were averaged to obtain daily values for the NovemberâDecemberâJanuaryâFebruary (NDJF) period. Forecasts were initialized on the first day of each month (November, December, January, and February) from 1981 to 2024 (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/seasonal-original-single-levels?tab=download), including both 1-month lead forecasts (e.g., November initialized in November) and longer lead times up to 4 months depending on the initialization month. Thus, the lead time varied by forecast start date, ranging from near-zero (same-month initialization) to approximately 4 months in advance. Additionally, monthly mean forecasts for the NDJF season were obtained from ECMWF for the same years from one to four-month lead times (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/seasonal-monthly-single-levels?tab=download). We chose ECMWF due to its widely recognized performance, as previous studies have established it as the gold standard for medium-range weather forecasting44.The ECMWF hindcasts are available at a spatial resolution of 1Â°âĂâ1Â° (~111âkm). However, at this coarse resolution, the model data are limited in their ability to capture temperature variations influenced by topography, proximity to coastlines, and other meso-climatic factors. In contrast, the observational dataset offers finer spatial detail and can represent these small-scale temperature variations more accurately, which is critical for realistic predictions of winter chill. To leverage the large-scale forecast signal while retaining fine-scale variability, we apply the ECMWF anomalies to the 4âkm observational climatology using both mean and SD scaling. In this approach, the coarse-scale forecast anomaly is applied across the high-resolution grid, while the local variability from observations is explicitly preserved. This SD-ratio scaling improves chill estimation relative to using raw forecasts or mean-only bias correction. Our aim is not to predict exact daily chill at individual locations, but to capture anomalies in chill sufficiency at monthly-to-seasonal scales for crop-growing regions. This method has been successfully applied in previous studies16,45,46.To compute CP, hourly temperatures are required, which were derived from daily maximum (Tmax) and minimum (Tmin) temperatures. The daily forecasts of Tmax and Tmin from ECMWF were bias-corrected using the nearest observational grid point and scaled through SD-ratio adjustment to match the observed variance.$${T}_{{max} {D}}^{{BC}}={T}_{max }^{{obsDClim}}+{T}_{max }^{{modDAnom}}\cdot \frac{{\sigma }_{{T}_{max D}}^{{obs}}}{{\sigma }_{{T}_{max D}}^{{mod}}}$$

(1) and (2), \({T}_{\max D}^{{{\mathrm{BC}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min D}^{{{\mathrm{BC}}}}\) represent the bias-corrected daily maximum and minimum temperatures from ECMWF daily forecasts at a spatial resolution of the observational data, around 4âkm for each lead time. The daily climatology \({T}_{\max }^{{{\mathrm{obsDClim}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min }^{{{\mathrm{obsDClim}}}}\) in the observational dataset were derived by averaging daily values for each calendar day over the period 1981â2024. \({T}_{\max }^{{{\mathrm{modDAnom}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min }^{{{\mathrm{modDAnom}}}}\) refer to the daily anomalies of ECMWFâs Tmax and Tmin, respectively. The daily anomalies were computed by subtracting the corresponding daily climatological means from the ECMWF hindcasts. The terms \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\max D}}^{{{\mathrm{obs}}}}\) and \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\min D}}^{{{\mathrm{obs}}}}\) denote the daily SDs of maximum and minimum temperatures from the observation, while \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\max D}}^{{{\mathrm{mod}}}}\) and \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\min D}}^{{{\mathrm{mod}}}}\) represent the corresponding SDs from the ECMWF forecasts. The daily SD of the corresponding variables was computed for each calendar day over the entire period.Since the observational data were available at a finer spatial resolution (4âkm) compared to the coarser resolution of the ECMWF forecasts (1Â°âĂâ1Â°), it was necessary to align their resolutions to perform the arithmetic operations in the equation. To achieve this, we resampled the ECMWF forecasts to match the resolution of observational data by replicating each grid cell across the corresponding observational data grids.The monthly forecasts of Tmax and Tmin from ECMWF were then downscaled to daily values using observational data from the nearest grid point to each ECMWF grid cell. Bias and variance scaling (SD-ratio) was applied in this process to preserve the observed interannual variability while retaining the large-scale anomaly signal. (3) and (4), \({T}_{\max M}^{{{\mathrm{BC}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min M}^{{{\mathrm{BC}}}}\) refer to the daily maximum and minimum temperatures generated from ECMWF monthly forecasts at a spatial resolution of the observational data, 4âkm for each lead time. \({T}_{\max }^{{{\mathrm{modMAnom}}}}\) and \({T}_{\min }^{{{\mathrm{modMAnom}}}}\) are the monthly anomalies of Tmax and Tmin from ECMWF, calculated by subtracting their respective climatology from the ECMWF hindcasts. The terms \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\max M}}^{{{\mathrm{mod}}}}\) and \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\min M}}^{{{\mathrm{mod}}}}\) denote the monthly SDs of Tmax and Tmin from the ECMWF forecasts, while \({\sigma }_{{T}_{{{\mathrm{maxM}}}}}^{{{\mathrm{obs}}}}\) and \({\sigma }_{{T}_{\min M}}^{{{\mathrm{obs}}}}\) represent the corresponding SDs from the reference data.Hourly temperatures needed for chill computation were derived from the daily Tmax and Tmin, latitude, and sunrise and sunset times. Daytime warming was modeled using a sine curve, while nighttime cooling was modeled with a logarithmic function, following the methods in the ChillR package developed by Luedeling et al47. To evaluate whether deriving hourly temperatures from daily Tmax and Tmin introduces substantial uncertainty in chill accumulation, we computed annual total CP using both methods for three representative stations (Modesto, Davis, and Five Points) using California Irrigation Management System (CIMIS) data (https://cimis.water.ca.gov/). The annual totals from these two approaches were computed using paired t-tests and Wilcoxon tests (implemented in R), which indicated no significant differences, confirming that the derived hourly temperatures provided a reliable estimate of chill accumulation.Chill quantificationChill units are a thermal-time metric quantifying exposure to effective chilling temperatures, and dormancy is completed once sufficient chill units have accumulated48. It can be measured as one hour at an optimal temperature, as in the Chilling Hours model, or a more complex unit of accumulated chill, as in the Dynamic Model. The total chilling requirement reflects the number of units or hours accumulated within a specified temperature range, which varies by species and cultivars49. Chill accumulation can be quantified using the Chilling Hours model, which counts the number of hours with temperatures between 0âÂ°C and 7.2âÂ°C50, while ignoring all other temperatures, or by calculating CP using the dynamic model1,3. The dynamic model is considered particularly well-suited for warming climates, such as that of California, because it represents chill accumulation in a more physiologically based manner than simpler chill-hour models and partially accounts for the reduction of chilling effectiveness at higher temperatures51,52,53.We quantified chill accumulation using CP, calculated with the Dynamic_model function from the ChillR package47. The dynamic model estimates CP through a two-step process: first, exposure to cold temperatures leads to the formation of an intermediate product, which can either be destroyed by subsequent warm temperatures or strengthened by moderate temperatures. Once a specific quantity of this intermediate product accumulates, it is permanently converted into a CP, unaffected by later temperature changes. The model needs hourly temperature data (in degrees Celsius) over a specified time period and applies experimentally derived constants within an exponential equation to compute the cumulative CP across the entire duration.We computed CP for the NDJF season total, as well as monthly total for the entire California using observational data, and ECMWFâs monthly and daily hindcasts for the period 1981â2024. For each season, chill accumulation from November and December of a given year was combined with January and February of the following year (e.g., NovemberâDecember 1981 with JanuaryâFebruary 1982). Chill computation of the 2024â2025 season was not possible, as January and February data for 2025 were unavailable at the time of this study.Analysis of CPWe computed the slope of the interannual total CP in the observational data between 1980 and 2023. For each grid point, the trend slope of total CP was estimated using Senâs Slope Estimator20. This method computes the slope for each pair of data points as the ratio of the difference in chill values (y) to the difference in time (x). The final Senâs Slope is the median of all pairwise slopes, providing a robust estimate of trend magnitude that is less sensitive to outliers or non-normal distributions than a simple least squares linear fit. The statistical significance of the trends (1980â2023) was assessed using the autocorrelation-adjusted MannâKendall test (YueâPilon modification)21,22. Both the trend slope and modified significance were computed in Python using the pymannkendall package.A trend was considered statistically significant if the p-value was less than 0.05. We then clipped the crop-growing regions on the gridded chill data, to determine the proportion of each region experiencing significant chill decline. The proportion of each region experiencing a significant decline was calculated by dividing the number of significant grid cells within the region by the total number of grid cells in that region, then multiplying by 100. We also calculated chill for Southern California (south of 37Â° latitude) and for individual counties by averaging chill values across all grid cells within the latitude band or county boundaries.We computed the statistical significance of the difference in the coefficient of variation (CV) of CP between the first (1980â1989) and last (2014â2023) decades. The CV was computed separately for each period, and a statistical comparison was conducted using an independent t-test54,55. The difference was considered significant if the p-value was less than 0.05. Additionally, we assessed the interannual variability by computing the SD of CP using a 10-year moving window for each crop-growing region.To examine whether CMIP6 projections capture the observed decline in chill accumulation, we calculated CP using Tmax and Tmin projections from Global Climate Models (GCMs) participating in Coupled Model Intercomparison Project phase 6 (CMIP6) experiments for both historical (1981â2014) and future (2015â2100) periods for the entire California. We selected three GCMS â ACCESS-CM2, EC-Earth3, EC-Earth3-Vegâbased on their demonstrated effectiveness for the California region, as determined through process-based and statistical metric56. These models were also chosen because high-resolution (~3âkm) statistically downscaled projections are available. These CMIP6 GCMs typically operate at coarse spatial resolutions, they do not adequately capture fine-scale geographic variation critical for accurate chill estimation. Therefore, we used downscaled outputs of these selected GCMs generated through the Localized Constructed Analogs, version 2 (LOCA2)57, which provide improved spatial resolution (3âkm). The downscaled, gridded datasets for the period 2015â2100 are publicly available in NetCDF format through Cal-Adapt (https://cal-adapt.org/data/download/).For future projections, we used the high-emission scenario, Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP 5â8.5), which assumes continued growth in greenhouse gas emissions throughout the century58. Although multiple scenarios are commonly assessed in climate impact studies, we selected SSP 5â8.5 to enable a direct comparison with earlier winter chill assessments that used the highest emissions scenarios available at the time to establish the earliest plausible timeline of chill decline. Consistent with this objective, SSP 5â8.5 is used here to evaluate whether the upper-bound projected decline in winter chill is sufficient to explain the observed historical trends. Extending this analysis to multiple SSPs to assess scenario-dependent uncertainty remains an important direction for future work.We clipped these CMIP6 projected chill for each crop-growing region in Southern California and computed the average annual chill accumulation for each crop. We compared the number of years with insufficient chill in observed data and historical projections for the same period (1981â2014) to examine whether CMIP6 projections under- or overestimate the observed decline in chill. Also, we examined whether the continuous decline in chill is similar across all counties.The chill sufficiency thresholds for pistachio, walnut, cherry, and plum were adopted from Pope5. For each crop, chilling requirements vary substantially across cultivars, locations, years, and experimental methods, particularly for stone fruits59. We use a crop-specific chill threshold defined as the median across all varieties of a given crop reported in Pope5. We use the median of CP, since it is less sensitive to extremes and enables consistent regional-scale comparisons in the absence of county-level varietal data. These thresholds are treated as representative benchmarks rather than fixed physiological limits. Consequently, our results are interpreted in terms of increasing frequency and proximity of episodic chill insufficiency driven by interannual variability, rather than the precise timing of persistent threshold crossings inferred from long-term trends. While cultivars with higher-than-median chill requirements may face greater vulnerability and lower-chill cultivars may experience reduced risk, our objective is not to predict cultivar-specific outcomes but to characterize regional-scale chill risk and its temporal evolution under observed conditions, climate projections, and forecasts.To evaluate interannual predictive performance, we compared how many years in the observational record were correctly captured by ECMWF forecasts. Forecast accuracy was evaluated at two thresholds: predictions were considered correct if the predicted CP were within 10% or within 5% of the observed NDJF totals, since large overestimates could misrepresent chill sufficiency. Although crops may tolerate up to a 20% shortfall, we adopted this conservative threshold to offer more robust forecasts of chill conditions25.We evaluated the accuracy of chill forecasts derived from bias-corrected ECMWF hindcasts initialized on the first day of each month (November through February), using both daily and monthly mean products. The analysis included forecasts with lead times ranging from 1 to 4 months, depending on initialization month. Forecast skill in predicting chill was assessed at a shorter 1-month lead, with February forecasts first, followed by comparing longer lead time forecasts.We performed this analysis at the crop-growing region level in Southern California (<37Â° latitude) by clipping the chill data to each region and calculating regional averages. Also, we performed the analysis at the individual grid level. Additionally, we calculated the ACC for each grid between the ECMWF-derived chill and that computed from observations.We also assessed the extent to which interannual variability in winter chill accumulation is influenced by the El NiĂ±oâSouthern Oscillation (ENSO). To do this, we used the ONI from NOAAâs Climate Prediction Center (CPC), which is based on three-month rolling averages of sea surface temperature (SST) anomalies in the NiĂ±o 3.4 region (5Â° Nâ5Â° S, 120Â°â170Â° W), derived from the Extended Reconstructed SST dataset, version 5 (ERSST.v5) (https://origin.cpc.ncep.noaa.gov/products/analysis_monitoring/ensostuff/ONI_v5.php). We then computed both lead and lag correlations between the NiĂ±o 3.4 index and CP to evaluate the temporal relationship between ENSO phases and chill variability.

Data availability
              
              We used several publicly available datasets in this study. GRIDMET meteorological data are available from the Northwest Knowledge Network (https://www.northwestknowledge.net/metdata/data/). CMIP6 downscaled climate projections (LOCA2) were obtained via the Intake ESM catalog (https://cadcat.s3.amazonaws.com/cae-collection.json). Crop distribution data were obtained from the USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Cropland Data Layer (https://croplandcros.scinet.usda.gov/) as GeoTIFF files and processed into crop-specific spatial masks using ArcGIS Pro. ECMWF seasonal forecast data (daily and monthly products) were obtained from the Copernicus Climate Data Store (https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/). All climate and crop datasets were spatially aligned on a common latitudeâlongitude grid (WGS84), and crop-specific grid cells were used to extract climate variables for subsequent analysis. Processed datasets used to generate the figures are publiclyÂ available on Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20058559. Figure-specific datasets include GRIDMET-derived chill time series, CMIP6 historical and SSP585 projections, ECMWF bias-corrected and monthly-derived chill products, and crop distribution and location datasets used for spatial aggregation and visualization.
            Code availability
              
              All chill computations were performed in R (v4.3.3) using open-source geospatial and climate analysis libraries. Specifically, chill calculations for GRIDMET, ECMWF, and CMIP6 datasets were implemented using the chillR package, applying the Dynamic Model after conversion of daily temperature inputs to hourly temperature using chillR::make_hourly_temps. NetCDF handling and supporting data processing steps within R were performed using the ncdf4, terra, dplyr, and tidyr packages. R workflows were parallelized using furrr and future for efficient grid-cell-level computation. All other data processing, geospatial workflows, and analysis pipelines were implemented in Python (v3.11.11). This included GRIDMET, ECMWF, and CMIP6 preprocessing, spatial mapping, observationalâmodel comparison workflows, crop-specific time series computation, and statistical analyses. Python-based workflows relied on open-source packages including NumPy, Pandas, GeoPandas, Xarray, SciPy, Matplotlib, and Shapely. All model-specific preprocessing steps are implemented in the scripts corresponding to each analysis component. Final integration of R-derived chill outputs and Python-processed datasets was used to generate all reported results, including regional (south of 37Â° N) and grid-level performance metrics. All scripts were developed in-house and follow a fully reproducible workflow using consistent input datasets. All code used in this study is publicly available at: https://github.com/pkj002/califo_work/tree/main/CommSust.

items.title: VOIHotels e WWF insieme per unâospitalitĂ  piĂč attenta alla natura
items.description:
Prosegue nel 2026 la collaborazione fra il WWF Italia e VOIhotels, la divisione alberghiera di Alpitour World
The post VOIHotels e WWF insieme per un&
items.body: In evidenza WWF Italia: 60 anni per la natura In evidenza Il Panda Siamo Noi In evidenza L'orso bruno marsicano In evidenza Adotta una specie Prosegue nel 2026 la collaborazione fra il WWF Italia e VOIhotels, la divisione alberghiera di Alpitour World. Il nuovo ciclo di attivitĂ  si sviluppa nellâambito di âDestinazione Naturaâ, il nome attribuito alla collaborazione per il 2026 tra WWF Italia e VOIhotels, che raccoglie sotto unâunica cornice le principali aree di lavoro condivise, dalla gestione della plastica alle attivitĂ  educative e al coinvolgimento degli ospiti. Con i brand VOIhotels e VRetreats, la catena alberghiera conta oggi 27 strutture tra resort e hotel in Italia e allâestero e accoglie oltre 1,6 milioni di ospiti allâanno. La collaborazione con il WWF Italia, che coinvolge le 19 strutture VOIhotels sul territorio italiano, contribuisce a rafforzare la responsabilitĂ  aziendale nelle pratiche quotidiane e nel dialogo con un pubblico ampio e diversificato. Le prioritĂ  Tra le prioritĂ  di questâanno Ăš previsto lâavvio di un lavoro strutturato sul tema plastica e packaging. Il WWF Italia affiancherĂ  VOIhotels in un percorso volto alla definizione di una policy aziendale e di un piano dâazione con obiettivi misurabili per ridurne lâimpronta plastica e contrastare i rischi di dispersione in natura. Lâiniziativa agirĂ  su piĂč livelli, coinvolgerĂ  diverse funzioni aziendali e sarĂ  accompagnata da momenti di formazione interna e dalla condivisione di contenuti informativi che coinvolgeranno anche gli ospiti. La collaborazione prosegue nel 2026 anche sul fronte educativo, con il rafforzamento delle attivitĂ  dedicate ai piĂč giovani. Dopo le esperienze avviate lo scorso anno, Ăš prevista la realizzazione presso il VOI Tanka Village in Sardegna di uno spazio dedicato allâincontro autentico con la natura. Non una semplice area giochi, ma un percorso sensoriale tra specie autoctone e installazioni interattive. Qui i giovani ospiti potranno imparare a leggere il territorio, scoprendo i segreti della flora e della fauna locale e lâimportanza della conservazione, per tornare a casa con una nuova consapevolezza e apprendendo il rispetto per il mondo naturale. OperativitĂ  e sensibilizzazione Nel corso della stagione 2026, VOIhotels attiverĂ  inoltre iniziative di raccolta fondi che offriranno agli ospiti la possibilitĂ  di sostenere volontariamente le attivitĂ  istituzionali del WWF Italia, diventando parte attiva dellâimpegno ambientale della catena alberghiera. Nel complesso, la collaborazione si articola come un percorso di lavoro che unisce operativitĂ  e sensibilizzazione, con unâattenzione particolare alla definizione di azioni concrete. Lâobiettivo Ăš fareÂ  del viaggio anche unâoccasione di consapevolezza, portando contenuti legati alla tutela della natura allâinterno dellâesperienza di soggiorno, in modo coerente con lâospitalitĂ .

items.title: Omaggio a Sir David Attenborough
items.description: David Attenborough compie 100 anni lâ8 maggio 2026: un traguardo che non riguarda solo la sua straordinaria longevitĂ , ma anche lâimpatto che ha avuto
items.body:
Home
                    
                            
                            
                                                                    
                        News ed Eventi

                                                Omaggio a Sir David Attenborough

Omaggio a Sir David Attenborough

Tag:
                                                                            Informazione ambientale                                                                    
                                                    
                                    
            
            
            
                
                                            Pubblicato il:
                        
                            8 Maggio 2026

Condividi

Condividi
            
                
                    
                
            
        
        
            
                
                    
                        
                    
                    Condividi su Facebook
                
            
            
                
                    
                        
                    
                    Condividi su X

                    Condividi su Whatsapp

Compie 100 anni il divulgatore naturalistico David Attenborough che ha insegnato a milioni di persone a guardare al mondo come un ecosistema complesso dove la sfera biologica e quella inorganica condividono un destino comune

David Attenborough compie 100 anni lâ8 maggio 2026: un traguardo che non riguarda solo la sua straordinaria longevitĂ , ma anche lâimpatto che ha avuto nel portare la scienza, la natura e la crisi climatica al grande pubblico. Sir David Attenborough Ăš stato il pioniere britannico della divulgazione naturalistica: per decenni, con i suoi documentari Ăš stato fonte di ispirazione per molte generazioni di appassionati di tutto il mondo. Â Naturalista e voce inconfondibile della natura per la BBC, Sir David Attenborough, vanta una carriera che dura da oltre settanta anni. Laureato in Scienze Naturali a Cambridge, nel 1976, iniziĂČ la produzione di Vita sulla Terra. Fu la prima serie naturalistica di questo genere che rivoluzionĂČ il modo di raccontare la vita sulla Terra. Il viaggio triennale intorno al mondo, comprese la visita di 40 paesi e le riprese di 600 specie. Seguirono poi le serie come Life on Earth, The Private Life of Plants e The Blue Planet, e nel 2025 vinse il Daytime Emmy Award come âPersonalitĂ  dellâanno per un programma non quotidianoâ, diventando cosĂŹ il piĂčÂ anziano vincitore nella storia del prestigioso premio televisivo. Â Un anno fa, nel 2025 ha presentato Ocean, un documentario di lungometraggio definito da lui stesso come âuno dei progetti piĂč importanti della sua carrieraâ, con lâobiettivo dichiarato di contribuire alla salvaguardia della biodiversitĂ  e al contrasto del cambiamento climatico.Â  Il documentarista che dopo aver trascorso una vita intera a filmare la terra, ha commentato che il luogo piĂč importante non Ăš sulla terra ma nel mare: Â âse salviamo il mare, salviamo il nostro mondoâ.
Negli ultimi anni, la sua voce si Ăš fatta piĂč urgente. Gli ultimi documentari come A Life on Our Planet o Extinction: The Facts affrontano apertamente i disastri ambientali causati dallâuomo. Attenborough riesce ancora a farsi ascoltare perchĂ© parla di soluzioni, non solo di problemi. Â Tra i suoi interventi piĂč memorabili câĂš quello alla COP26 di Glasgow, in Scozia nel 2021, quando parlĂČ ai leader mondiali con un appello netto: la crisi climatica non era piĂč un problema lontano, ma una sfida presente, urgente e collettiva. In quellâoccasione ricordĂČ che siamo giĂ  nei guai e che la via dâuscita passava da unâazione comune, rapida e concreta per fermare le emissioni e proteggere il futuro. Nel suo intervento Attenborough sottolineĂČ alcuni punti chiave: la stabilitĂ  climatica che ha permesso lo sviluppo della civiltĂ  umana, la necessitĂ  di ridurre rapidamente le emissioni di carbonio e lâurgenza di mantenere vivo lâobiettivo di evitare lâaumento della temperatura di 1,5 Â°C. Il suo discorso era insieme scientifico ed etico: non chiedeva solo politiche migliori, ma una visione condivisa del destino umano.Â  Una delle frasi piĂč forti del suo messaggio fu che, se divisi siamo stati abbastanza potenti da danneggiare il pianeta, uniti possiamo essere abbastanza forti da salvarlo. Ă un passaggio cruciale perchĂ© trasforma il cambiamento climatico da questione tecnica a scelta di civiltĂ , chiamando in causa governi, imprese e cittadini.Â  âRicordare Attenborough Ăš importante perchĂ© il suo messaggio riesce ancora oggi a parlare a tutte le generazioni in modo trasversale. Il suo valore sta nellâaver reso accessibile un tema complesso senza semplificarlo, unendo dati e capacitĂ  narrativa in un linguaggio comprensibile ed aver insegnato a guardare al mondo come un ecosistema complesso che accomuna in un unico destino sfera biologica e sfera inorganicaâ, commenta Pietro Rubellini, direttore generale di ARPAT. Â âĂ ancora oggi una fonte di ispirazione perchĂ© mostra come lâautorevolezza e la divulgazione possono essere utilizzate per coinvolgere e responsabilizzare un pubblico ampio â prosegue Rubellini -. Â A cento anni, la sua voce continua a ricordare che la difesa della natura non Ăš un gesto simbolico, ma un patto concreto di solidarietĂ  tra generazioniâ.
E il valore piĂč grande del suo intervento alla COP26 Ăš lâaver esplicitato ai leader del mondo che il tempo dellâattesa Ăš finito, ma che una soluzione Ăš ancora possibile se lâumanitĂ  agisce insieme. Festeggiare i suoi 100 anni significa anche rinnovare lâimpegno che ha chiesto al mondo: proteggere il clima, difendere la biodiversitĂ  e lasciare ai piĂč giovani non solo parole, ma un pianeta ancora vivibile.Â  Il discorso pronunciato da Sir David Attenborough alla COP26 di Glasgow il 1Â° novembre 2021 rimane uno dei momenti piĂč iconici della diplomazia climatica moderna. In qualitĂ  di portavoce dei cittadini, Attenborough si rivolse ai leader mondiali con un appello che univa rigore scientifico e un profondo senso di urgenza morale.
Di seguito, riportiamo alcuni dei passaggi salienti del suo appello finale in Scozia, in cui richiama tutti allâazione: âDopotutto, siamo i piĂč grandi risolutori di problemi che siano mai esistiti sulla Terra. Ora capiamo questo problema. âŠ. Dobbiamo fermare le emissioni di carbonio in questo decennio. Dobbiamo recuperare miliardi di tonnellate di carbonio dallâaria. Una nuova rivoluzione industriale, alimentata da milioni di innovazioni sostenibili, Ăš essenziale ed Ăš giĂ  iniziataââŠ âCondivideremo tutti i benefici: energia pulita e accessibile, aria sana e cibo a sufficienza per sostenerci tutti. La natura Ăš un alleato fondamentale. Ogni volta che ripristineremo la natura, essa recupererĂ  il carbonio e ci aiuterĂ  a riportare lâequilibrio sul nostro pianeta. Se divisi siamo stati abbastanza potenti da destabilizzare il nostro pianeta, sicuramente, lavorando insieme siamo abbastanza potenti da salvarloâ â e conclude â âNella mia vita, ho assistito a un terribile declino. Nel vostro, potreste e dovreste assistere a una meravigliosa guarigione. Quella speranza disperata, signore e signori, delegati, eccellenze, Ăš il motivo per cui il mondo sta guardando a voi e perchĂ© voi siete quiâ.
Questo discorso Ăš considerato una pietra miliare perchĂ© sposta il focus dalla pura contabilitĂ  delle emissioni a una questione di responsabilitĂ  intergenerazionale. Attenborough ha sottolineato che, sebbene la nostra specie sia stata capace di causare danni senza precedenti, possiede anche le competenze e lâingegno per invertire la rotta. La sua visione funge da promemoria costante per i decisori politici: la lotta al cambiamento climatico non Ăš unâopzione tra le tante, ma una necessitĂ  per garantire la sopravvivenza della civiltĂ  umana e la protezione della biosfera. La testimonianza di David Attenborough non Ăš solo un racconto del cambiamento climatico ma un invito potente a sperare e agire: ci ricorda che possiamo davvero cambiare il mondo e ci offre una guida su come farlo: proteggere la biodiversitĂ , riforestare, abbracciare le energie rinnovabili e ripensare le nostre abitudini. Ă un messaggio di speranza e responsabilitĂ  che puĂČ ispirare ogni persona che viva su questo pianeta.
Foto di Pexels da Pixabay

items.title: Goodbye GDP? 31 ways to replace the world's favourite measure of economic health
items.body:
NEWS
                08 May 2026

Goodbye GDP? 31 ways to replace the world's favourite measure of economic health

Proposed UN progress indicators include greenhouse gas emissions, life expectancy and children's performance in reading and maths.
                
            
        
        
            
                By

Mariana Lenharo0

Mariana Lenharo
                
                
                    
                        
                            Mariana Lenharo is a reporter for Nature in New York City.
                        
                    
                
                
                    
                        
                            View author publications
                            
                                
                                    Search author on:
                                    PubMed
                                    Â Google Scholar

Email
            
        
    
    
        
            Bluesky
            
        
    
    
        
            Facebook

LinkedIn

Reddit

Whatsapp

X

Beyond GDP indicators include measures of air (pictured here in Shanghai).Credit: Jackyenjoyphotography/GettyThe United Nations is considering 31 new indicators to âcomplement and go beyondâ the worldâs main measure of economic growth, gross domestic product (GDP).The proposed new annual indicators include economic metrics such as household disposable income per person, and environmental data such as a countryâs greenhouse gas emissions and levels of particulate matter in the air. Also included are health and education indicators such as life expectancy and childrenâs performance in reading and maths; as well as measures of wellbeing such as the proportion of women and girls subjected to physical and/or sexual violence. Fifteen of the 31 proposed indicators are already part of indicators for the UNâs Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).The new indicators are in a report, Counting What Counts, written by a multidisciplinary committee of researchers and policymakers, co- chaired by economists Kaushik Basu, based at Cornell University in Ithaca, New York, and Nora Lustig at Tulane University in New Orleans. The group was assembled last year by UN Secretary General AntĂłnio Guterres. Attempts to dethrone GDP, including by the UN, go back at least three decades but this is the first time that a UN chief has responded to such a request from member states. Speaking at a launch event at UN headquarters in New York City on 7 May, Guterres called the report a âlandmark step in correcting a longstanding blind spot in measuring progressâ.What GDP is â and isnâtThere are different ways to measure GDP. According to one widely-used method, it is the sum of what a countryâs households and governments spend and what businesses invest. The rules for how it is calculated are agreed by the UN Statistical Commission, which is a kind of parliament of the worldâs national statistical agencies. But data are compiled and reported by individual countries. It is mentioned in numerous laws and is used as a benchmark in policymaking worldwide. Countries regularly report their defence, health and research spending as a percentage of GDP.GDP growth is also often the main objective of a countryâs economic policy. âIt is now being used in ways its architects never intended,â Guterres said. âIn my time as secretary-general, the size of the global economy has risen over 50 per cent, adjusting for inflation.â At the same time, many indicators for health, biodiversity, job-creation, human rights, peace âwith conflicts now at levels not seen since World War IIâ, Guterres added, are going backwards.Annalena Baerbock, UN General Assembly president, added that GDP increases when there are harms. âIf many people in your country have accidents on the road or a chemical disaster, also this would at that moment increase your GDP because you have to do something to counter it.âMixed signalsThe report has drawn mixed reactions from researchers and policymakers.Rutger Hoekstra, who studies economic, environmental and social indicators at Leiden University in the Netherlands, describes GDP as âa flawed indicator of economic growthâ. He adds that it's important to see wellbeing, inclusion and sustainability recognized in the new plan as these âare superior measures of societal progressâ.Paula Caballero, regional director for Latin America at The Nature Conservancy, a conservation non-profit organization based in Arlington, Virginia, adds that the initiative has been a long time in the making. Caballero, a former diplomat from Colombia, was instrumental (with colleague Patti LondoĂ±o) in establishing the SDGs .She says that discussions about moving beyond GDP were part of the SDGsâ negotiations, but that the idea was dropped as there were too many items on the agenda. GDP, she adds is âvery short sightedâ, as it does not account for the impacts of future growth, such as from climate change.Stephen Polasky, an environmental economist at the University of Minnesota in Saint Paul, points out that some of the proposed environmental measures, notably biodiversity and natural capital, are limited. âI don't object to anything that is included,â he says, but adds that there should have been more detail on the nature-related indicators. âInclusion of biodiversity/environmental issues should be a major issue for a report that looks to go beyond GDP. It seems like a missed opportunity.â

items.title: Happy 100th birthday David Attenborough! Nature salutes you
items.body:
EDITORIAL
                08 May 2026

Happy 100th birthday David Attenborough! Nature salutes you

The science communicator has raised awareness of the natural world and its myriad interconnections on which humans depend.
                
            
        
        
    
    
        
    
        
            Email
            
        
    
    
        
            Bluesky
            
        
    
    
        
            Facebook

LinkedIn

Reddit

Whatsapp

X

Filming for the 1995 series The Private Life of Plants on Ellesmere Island, Canada. Credit: Neil Nightingale/NPL

You have full access to this article via your institution.

It is one of the most memorable moments in natural-history film-making: two young mountain gorillas playing with the broadcaster David Attenborough in Rwanda, later joined by the gorillasâ watchful mother. Attenborough is in his element, quietly demolishing a widely held perception â bolstered over decades by films such as King Kong â that these shy and peaceful creatures pose a violent threat to humans. The clip, filmed in 1978 for the landmark BBC series Life on Earth: A Natural History, demonstrates what has become Attenboroughâs trademark over his 70-year career: the communication of new, surprising and complex phenomena by showing rather than telling. Thereâs no lecture here â just curiosity, mischief and arresting visual impact. Attenborough turns 100 on 8 May. Here, Nature salutes him and celebrates his unparalleled impact as a science communicator who has brought nature closer to many millions of people. We also pay tribute in an immersive article that shows his extraordinary life in pictures (see go.nature.com/4wddktw). The inspiration he provides must continue to shape efforts to conserve the natural world. A scientist and communicatorItâs hard to overstate Attenboroughâs influence on science broadcasting. In the early decades of television, programmes fronted by experts usually involved men giving lectures to the camera, with photography and graphics having a secondary role. Attenborough was no less an expert and, unusually for someone at the BBC at the time, had studied science: geology and zoology at the University of Cambridge, UK. But he brought a more involved, engaging approach, getting out into the field. His films are a personal journey of discovery, but the spotlight is shared. A life in pictures: celebrating David Attenborough at 100Life on Earth, a 13-part series of hour-long episodes that aired in 1979, told a sweeping story of the origins and evolution of life. It took three years to make, and Attenborough and his crew travelled 2.1 million kilometres to 100 locations in 49 countries, filming 650 species. In letting the cameras (and his now-familiar enthused whisper) do the talking, he took full advantage of the beginning of colour television â an innovation he had been instrumental in bringing to the United Kingdom, during a stint as the controller of the television channel BBC2 in the 1960s. The series was watched by an estimated 500 million people worldwide â more than one-tenth of the global population at the time.Many of the scenes in Life on Earth had never before been captured on camera, and the series spotlighted not just charismatic megafauna, but also the wonders of life at all scales. One of the most memorable segments involves Darwinâs frogs (Rhinoderma darwinii), a tiny species found in the forests of Chile and Argentina. The males collect and incubate fertilized eggs in their mouths, before dramatically releasing the newly developed froglets.We are all broadcasters nowAttenborough followed Life on Earth with ever-more-ambitious films on subjects such as mammals, birds and life in the deep ocean, culminating in two further landmark series: Planet Earth and Blue Planet, which he narrated well into his later years. For much of this time, only relatively few well-financed broadcasters had the resources to take on such projects. The BBCâs budget for Life on Earth reached an estimated ÂŁ1 million (then equivalent to around US$2 million), according to Jean-Baptiste Gouyon, a science-communication researcher at University College London who has studied Attenboroughâs documentaries (J.-B. Guoyon BBC Wildlife Documentaries in the Age of Attenborough, 2019). It was an astronomical sum at the time. Today, public-service broadcasters such as the BBC are under considerable financial and political pressure, and so are less likely to take on such risky projects.Streaming platforms such as Disney+ and Netflix are taking up the baton, satisfying to an extent the market for big-ticket nature documentaries created by Attenborough. But as technology has evolved, so, too, have opportunities to become creators. Camera equipment that was once carted around in trucks and vans now fits in our pockets. Content creators on YouTube or TikTok have the potential to achieve Attenborough levels of viewership â and engagement â regardless of resourcing or where they are in the world. âPowerful and horrifyingâ â David Attenboroughâs latest film is a cry to protect our oceansThere has never been a greater need for such science communication and engagement. Attenboroughâs long career has coincided with a period during which humanityâs impact on the planet has vastly increased. His evolving output reflects changing attitudes towards those effects. The first series he presented was Zoo Quest in the 1950s, in which a team from London Zoo collected species from other countries and transported them to London. It would not be commissioned today. Series such as Life on Earth have been criticized for focusing on celebrating the wonders of evolution and the beauty of natural environments, while excluding many people who live in the locations involved, and overlooking threats to such places from unchecked industrial development powered by fossil fuels. By contrast, Attenboroughâs newer films have broadened their focus to include not only species, but also ecosystems â biodiversity in its fullest sense. They have also adopted a more urgent tone about the degradation of the environment; his 2025 film Ocean with David Attenborough, for example, includes heart-wrenching footage of the devastation wrought on ocean-floor ecosystems by the fishing practice of bottom trawling.And, after many decades of avoiding the lecture format and giving his own opinion, Attenborough has increasingly turned to personal advocacy, warning of the risks of climate change. In a 2021 speech to the United Nations COP26 climate meeting in Glasgow, UK, he told delegates: âIn my lifetime Iâve witnessed a terrible decline. In yours, you could and should witness a wonderful recovery.â Attenborough has arguably done more than anyone else on the planet to raise awareness of the natural world and its myriad interconnections on which humans depend. As we mark his centenary, the most fitting legacy of the next 100 years would be for humanity to achieve that necessary recovery.
